--- SOURCE 1: Reimagining The Western - Number Analytics ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/reimagining-the-western

SUMMARY:
The traditional Western narrative has been criticized for its narrow and often stereotypical portrayals of the American West. Critics argue that the genre has been dominated by white, male perspectives, neglecting the experiences of women, people of color, and other marginalized communities .



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Western-centric narratives - (Intro to Comparative Literature) - Fiveable ---
URL: https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-to-comparative-literature/western-centric-narratives

SUMMARY:
Western-centric narratives refer to perspectives and interpretations of history, culture, and society that prioritize Western experiences and viewpoints.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Unpacking Western Knowledge in Colonial Literature ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/western-knowledge-colonial-literature

SUMMARY:
On the other hand, literature also provided a platform for colonized peoples to voice their experiences and challenge dominant narratives.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 4: Western literature | History, Characteristics, Books ... - Britannica ---
URL: https://www.britannica.com/art/Western-literature

SUMMARY:
Hebrew culture exerted its greatest literary influence on the West because of the place held by its early writings as the Old Testament of the Christian Bible; and this literature profoundly influenced Western consciousness through translation from about the time of St. Augustine onward into every vernacular language as well as into Latin. Medieval, “belonging to the Middle Ages,” is used here to refer to the literature of Europe and the eastern Mediterranean from as early as the establishment of the Eastern Roman, or Byzantine, Empire about ad 300 for medieval Greek, from the period following upon the fall of Rome in 476 for medieval Latin, and from about the time of Charlemagne and the Carolingian Renaissance he fostered in France (c.

 Top Questions 

*   What is Western literature?    
*   Which regions are primarily associated with Western literature?

 Show more  Show less 

**Western literature**, history of literatures in the languages of the Indo-European family, along with a small number of other languages whose [cultures](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/cultures) became closely associated with the West, from ancient times to the present.

[Diverse](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Diverse) as they are, European literatures, like European languages, are parts of a common heritage. Greek, Latin, Germanic, Baltic and Slavic, Celtic, and [Romance languages](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Romance-languages) are all members of the Indo-European family. (Finnish and Hungarian and [Semitic languages](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Semitic-languages) of the eastern Mediterranean, such as Hebrew, are not Indo-European. Literatures in these languages are, however, closely associated with major Western literatures and are often included among them.) The common literary heritage is essentially that originating in ancient Greece and Rome. It was preserved, transformed, and spread by Christianity and thus transmitted to the [vernacular](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/vernacular) languages of the European Continent, the [Western Hemisphere](https://www.britannica.com/place/Western-Hemisphere), and other regions that were settled by Europeans. To the present day, this body of writing displays a unity in its main features that sets it apart from the literatures of the rest of the world. Such common characteristics are considered here.

For specific information about the major national literatures or literary traditions of the West, see such articles as [American literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/American-literature), [English literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/English-literature), [German literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/German-literature), [Greek literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/Greek-literature), [Latin American literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/Latin-American-literature), and [Scandinavian literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/Scandinavian-literature). Various other Western literatures—including those in the [Armenian](https://www.britannica.com/art/Armenian-literature), [Bulgarian](https://www.britannica.com/art/Bulgarian-literature), [Estonian](https://www.britannica.com/art/Estonian-literature), [Lithuanian](https://www.britannica.com/art/Lithuanian-literature), and [Romanian](https://www.britannica.com/art/Romanian-literature) languages—are also treated in separate entries.

Ancient literature
------------------

The stark fact about ancient Western literature is that the greater part of it has perished. Some of it had been forgotten before it was possible to commit it to writing; fire, war, and the ravages of time have robbed [posterity](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/posterity) of most of the rest; and the restitutions that archaeologists and paleographers achieve from time to time are small. Yet surviving writings in Greek and far more in Latin have included those that on ancient testimony marked the heights reached by the creative imagination and intellect of the ancient world.

Five ancient civilizations—Babylon and Assyria, Egypt, Greece, Rome, and the [culture](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/culture) of the Israelites in Palestine—each came into contact with one or more of the others. The two most ancient, Assyro-Babylonia, with its broken clay tablets, and [Egypt](https://www.britannica.com/art/Egyptian-art), with its rotted papyrus rolls, make no direct literary signal to the modern age; yet [Babylon](https://www.britannica.com/place/Babylonia) produced the first full code of laws and two epics of archetypal [myth](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/myth), which came to be echoed and re-echoed in distant lands, and Egypt’s mystical [intuition](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/intuition) of a supernatural world caught the imagination of the Greeks and Romans. [Hebrew](https://www.britannica.com/art/Hebrew-literature) culture exerted its greatest literary influence on the West because of the place held by its early writings as the [Old Testament](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Old-Testament) of the Christian Bible; and this literature profoundly influenced Western [consciousness](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/consciousness) through translation from about the time of St. Augustine onward into every vernacular language as well as into Latin. Until then, Judaism’s concentrated spirituality set it apart from the Greek and Roman world.

[![Image 23: Emily Dickinson (1830-1886) only confirmed photograph of Emily Dickinson. 1978 scan of a Daguerreotype. ca. 1847; in the Amherst College Archives. American poet. See Notes:](https://cdn.britannica.com/91/185691-131-0ACC5DF8/Emily-Dickinson-1847.jpg) Britannica Quiz Poetry: First Lines](https://www.britannica.com/quiz/poetry-first-lines)
Though influenced by the religious [myths](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/myths) of Mesopotamia, [Asia Minor](https://www.britannica.com/place/Anatolia), and Egypt, [Greek literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/Ancient-Greek-literature) has no direct literary ancestry and appears self-originated. Roman writers looked to Greek precept for themes, treatment, and choice of verse and meter. Rome eventually passed the torch on to the early Middle Ages, by which time Greek had been subsumed under a wholly [Latin](https://www.britannica.com/art/Latin-literature) tradition and was only rediscovered in its own right at the Renaissance—the “classical” tradition afterward becoming a threat to natural literary development, particularly when certain critics of the 17th century began to insist that the subjects and style of [contemporary](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/contemporary) writing should conform with those employed by Greece and Rome.

All of the chief [kinds of literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/genre-literature)—epic, tragedy, comedy, lyric, [satire](https://www.britannica.com/art/satire), history, [biography](https://www.britannica.com/art/biography-narrative-genre), and prose narrative—were established by the Greeks and Romans, and later developments have for the most part been secondary extensions. The Greek [epic](https://www.britannica.com/art/epic) of [Homer](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Homer-Greek-poet) was the model for the Latin of Virgil; the lyric fragments of [Alcaeus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Alcaeus) and [Sappho](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Sappho-Greek-poet) were echoed in the work of [Catullus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Catullus) and Ovid; the history of Thucydides was succeeded by that of [Livy](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Livy) and Tacitus; but the tragedy of the great Athenians of the 5th century bc had no worthy counterpart in Roman [Seneca](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Lucius-Annaeus-Seneca-Roman-philosopher-and-statesman) nor had the philosophical writings of Plato and Aristotle in those of any ancient Roman, for the practical Romans were not philosophers. Whereas Greek writers excelled in abstraction, the Romans had an unusually concrete vision and, as their art of portraiture shows, were intensely interested in human individuality.

![Image 24](https://cdn.britannica.com/marketing/BlueThistle.webp)

[![Image 25: See the portrayal of Odysseus (or Ulysses), as represented in literature, art, and cinema over the ages](https://cdn.britannica.com/00/186400-138-0BDA8D0D/Odysseus-literature-art-cinema.jpg?w=800&h=450&c=crop)](https://www.britannica.com/video/Odysseus-literature-art-cinema/-205668)

[See all videos for this article](https://www.britannica.com/art/Western-literature/images-videos)

In sum, the work of these writers and others and perhaps especially that of Greek authors expresses the imaginative and [moral](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/moral) temper of Western humanity. It has helped to create his values and to hand on a tradition to distant generations. Homer’s epics extend their concern from the right treatment of strangers to behavior in situations of deep involvement among rival heroes, their foes, and the overseeing gods; the tragedies of [Aeschylus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aeschylus-Greek-dramatist) and [Sophocles](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Sophocles) are a [sublime](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/sublime) expression of humankind’s breakthrough into moral awareness of the human situation. Among Roman authors an elevated [Stoicism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Stoicism) stressing the sense of duty is common to many, from Naevius, Ennius, and Cato to [Virgil](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Virgil), [Horace](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Horace-Roman-poet), and Seneca. A human ideal is to be seen in the savage satire of [Juvenal](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Juvenal) and in [Anacreon’s](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Anacreon) songs of love and wine, as it is in the philosophical thought of Plato and Aristotle. It is given voice by a chorus of Sophocles, “Wonders are many, but none is more wonderful than man, the power that crosses the white sea. . . .” The human ideal held up in Greek and Latin literature, formed after civilization had emerged from earlier centuries of [barbarism](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/barbarism), was to be transformed, before the ancient world came to its close, into the spiritual ideal of Judeo-Christianity, whose writers foreshadowed [medieval](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/medieval) literature.

Medieval literature
===================

[Medieval](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Medieval), “belonging to the [Middle Ages](https://www.britannica.com/event/Middle-Ages),” is used here to refer to the [literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/literature) of Europe and the eastern Mediterranean from as early as the establishment of the Eastern Roman, or [Byzantine](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Byzantine), Empire about ad 300 for medieval Greek, from the period following upon the fall of Rome in 476 for medieval Latin, and from about the time of Charlemagne and the [Carolingian Renaissance](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Carolingian-Renaissance) he fostered in France (c. 800) to the end of the 15th century for most written [vernacular](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/vernacular) literatures.

[Christianity](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Christianity) and the church
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

The establishment of Christianity throughout the territories that had formed the Roman Empire meant that Europe was exposed to and tutored in the systematic approach to life, literature, and religion developed by the early Church Fathers. In the West, the fusion of Christian and classical philosophy formed the basis of the medieval habit of interpreting life symbolically. Through [St. Augustine](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Augustine), [Platonic](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Platonic) and Christian thought were reconciled: the permanent and uniform order of the Greek universe was given Christian form; nature became sacramental, a symbolic revelation of spiritual truth. [Classical literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/classical-literature) was invested with this same symbolism; exegetical, or interpretative, methods first applied to the Scriptures were extended as a general principle to classical and [secular](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/secular) writings. The allegorical or symbolic approach that found in [Virgil](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Virgil) a pre-Christian prophet and in the _Aeneid_ a narrative of the soul’s journey through life to paradise (Rome) belonged to the same tradition as [Dante’s](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Dante-Alighieri) allegorical [conception](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/conception) of himself and his journey in _The Divine Comedy_.

The church not only established the purpose of literature but preserved it. [St. Benedict’s](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Benedict-of-Nursia) monastery at Monte Cassino in Italy was established in 529, and other [monastic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/monasticism) centers of scholarship followed, particularly after the 6th- and 7th-century Irish missions to the Rhine and Great Britain and the Gothic missions up the Danube. These monasteries were able to preserve the only classical literature available in the West through times when Europe was being raided by Goths, Vandals, Franks, and, later, the Norse in [succession](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/succession). The classical Latin authors so preserved and the [Latin](https://www.britannica.com/art/Latin-literature) works that continued to be written predominated over vernacular works throughout most of the period. St. Augustine’s _City of God,_ the Venerable Bede’s _Ecclesiastical History,_ the Danish [chronicle](https://www.britannica.com/art/chronicle-literature) of [Saxo Grammaticus](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saxo-Grammaticus), for example, were all written in Latin, as were most major works in the fields of philosophy, theology, history, and [science](https://www.britannica.com/science/science).

[Vernacular](https://www.britannica.com/topic/vernacular) works and drama
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

The main literary values of the period are found in vernacular works. The pre-Christian literature of Europe belonged to an [oral tradition](https://www.britannica.com/art/oral-literature) that was reflected in the _Poetic Edda_ and the sagas, or heroic epics, of Iceland, the Anglo-Saxon _Beowulf,_ and the German _Song of Hildebrand._ These belonged to a common [Germanic](https://www.britannica.com/art/German-literature) alliterative tradition, but all were first recorded by Christian scribes at dates later than the historical events they relate, and the pagan elements they contain were fused with Christian thought and feeling. The mythology of [Icelandic literature](https://www.britannica.com/art/Icelandic-literature) was echoed in every Germanic language and clearly stemmed from a common European source. Only the Scandinavian texts, however, give a [coherent](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/coherent) account of the stories and personalities involved. Numerous ballads in different countries also reflect an earlier native tradition of oral recitation. Among the best known of the many [genres](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/genres) that arose in medieval vernacular literatures were the [romance](https://www.britannica.com/art/romance-literature-and-performance) and the [courtly love](https://www.britannica.com/art/courtly-love) lyric, both of which combined elements from popular oral traditions with those of more scholarly or refined literature and both derived largely from France. The romance used classical or Arthurian sources in a poetic narrative that replaced the heroic epics of feudal society, such as _The Song of Roland,_ with a chivalrous tale of knightly [valor](https://www.me

# 6 Story Arcs Define Western Literature, Data-Mining Study Reveals

## Discover the six story arcs in Western literature that shape our narratives, including rags to riches and Cinderella.

![](https://cdn.discovermagazine.com/assets/image/6802/shutterstock_103225520-s.webp)

### Newsletter

Sign up for our email newsletter for the latest science news

Almost the entirety of Western literature can be fit neatly into just six story arcs, according to a new data-mining study.

From the panoply of novels that Western society has produced, distinct narrative patterns emerge, and many attempts have been made to pin down the shape of a story and categorize a protagonist’s journey. French writer Georges Polti claims there are [36 different types](https://archive.org/details/thirtysixdramati00polt) dramatic stories, while others have counted [seven narrative arcs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Seven_Basic_Plots) or [20](http://www.writersdigest.com/writing-articles/by-writing-goal/write-first-chapter-get-started/20-master-plots).

But new research from the University of Vermont utilizing data-mining techniques suggests that the majority of the Western canon falls into one of six basic categories.

#### A Story’s Path

Researchers from the Computational Story Lab looked at over 1,700 books from [Project Gutenberg](https://www.gutenberg.org/) for their study, winnowing out books such as dictionaries or those with less than 150 downloads. They analyzed the content of each book by taking samples of text, what they called “windows”, from throughout the story. They used the aptly named [“hedonometer”](http://hedonometer.org/index.html) , also developed by the Computational Story Lab, to compile a list of over 10,000 words and rate them on a spectrum of positive to negative using Amazon’s Mechanical Turk service. They [published their results](http://arxiv.org/pdf/1606.07772v1.pdf) last month on arXiv.org.

Adding up these windows over the course of a whole book produced graphs of characters’ fortunes — the highs and lows — throughout a given novel, and generated a broad visualization of the arc the story takes. According to the researchers, theses are the six story arcs that appear time and time again in Western literature:

**“Rags to riches” (the story gets better over time);**

**“Man in a hole” (fortunes fall, but the protagonist bounces back);**

**“Cinderella” (there’s an initial rise in good fortunes, followed by a setback, but a happy ending)**

**“Tragedy” or “riches to rags” (things only get worse);**

**“Oedipus” (bad luck, followed by promise, ending in a final fall)**

**“Icarus” (opens with good fortunes, but doomed to fail)**

![](https://cdn.discovermagazine.com/assets/image/6803/Screen-Shot-2016-07-06-at-1.38.41-PM-1024x822-s.jpg "Screen-Shot-2016-07-06-at-1.38.41-PM")

The six main story arcs in Western literature. From top left: rags to riches, man in a hole, Cinderella, tragedy, Oedipus, Icarus. (Credit: Reagan et. al/ University of Vermont)

Reagan et. al/ University of Vermont

The six main story arcs in Western literature. From top left: rags to riches, man in a hole, Cinderella, tragedy, Oedipus, Icarus. *(Credit: Reagan et. al/ University of Vermont)*

While some stories don’t fit into these archetypes, the researchers say that the majority of Western classics fall into one of these categories. The “man in a hole” and rags-to-riches storylines seemed to be the most prevalent, depending on which statistical technique they applied to the data.

The researchers do note that their technique will only track broad changes in emotional valence over time, ignoring shifts that occur on the level of the sentence or paragraph. For example, they provide a detailed breakdown of *Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows,* which doesn’t fit neatly into any one of the categories.

![](https://cdn.discovermagazine.com/assets/image/6804/Screenshot-2016-07-06-14.05.49-1024x476-s.jpg "Screenshot-2016-07-06-14.05.49")

Screenshot-2016-07-06-14.05.49-1024x476

However, the seven-book series, when taken as a whole, produces a more definite “rags to riches” story that fits in with established arcs.

In addition, their process cannot separate the fortunes of multiple characters, which could be a problem for books with multiple story lines — their program would definitely struggle with something as complex as *Game of Thrones*. Instead, their algorithm lumps the characters into one and tracks the overall emotional tone of the book from beginning to end.

#### How We Talk About Ourselves

The Gutenberg Collection is a compendium of classic works, however, more modern stories were not sampled. To call back to *Game of Thrones* again, there are many modern novels that tell more complex tales and which embrace emotional ambiguity, muddying the story’s arc and making a precise definition difficult.

In addition, the researchers looked only at works from the Western canon — analyzing stories from other cultures may produce very different trends and hint at diverse preferences. They say that they hope to include novels from other countries in future studies.

While their work hints at some of the broader patterns of thought that dominate Western culture, the researchers say that it could also help to teach computers how to communicate better. Teaching artificial intelligence to construct stories that follow popular arcs could allow them to form better arguments and relate concepts with more accuracy. The AI authors out there, even those [trained on Shakespeare](http://www.slate.com/articles/technology/future_tense/2016/07/the_difference_between_robot_generated_art_and_human_generated_art_is_that.html), fall somewhat short of engaging — or even comprehensible. Reading the stories that emerge from a particular culture gives unique insights into norms, practices and overall patterns of thought. If we want to teach computers to think like us, they’ll need to understand how we see the world.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Western narratives dominate literary discourse, often overshadowing diverse global perspectives, as seen in traditional Western literature's focus on white, male protagonists and experiences.

Non-Western voices are frequently marginalized in global literature, limiting representation and perpetuating cultural stereotypes, as highlighted by the lack of visibility for authors from diverse backgrounds.
Literature often reinforces stereotypes, with simplistic portrayals of cultures and identities, contributing to misconceptions and limiting the understanding of complex human experiences.
